texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Industrial CO2 emissions are an environmental problem not just for the power sector but also for non-power industries such as the cement, steel and oil re fi ning sectors. To handle this environmental issue, various technologies have been suggested to capture the emitted CO2. Chemical absorption using amine solvents is considered the most mature technology available and it has been demonstrated through a number of pilot plants such as SaskPower's Boundary Dam power station pilot plant (scale: 4 tCO2 captured/day) [1], the EU project CASTOR pilot plant, the TAURON Laziska pilot plant (scale: 1 tCO2 captured/day) [2], the Brindisi Pilot Plant (scale: 60 tCO2 captured/day) [3] and the Korea Electric Power Research Institute pilot plant (scale: 2 tCO2 captured/day) [4].
texts.text: Industrial CO2 emissions are an environmental problem not just for the power sector but also for non-power industries such as the cement, steel and oil re fi ning sectors. To handle this environmental issue, various technologies have been suggested to capture the emitted CO2. Chemical absorption using amine solvents is considered the most mature technology available and it has been demonstrated through a number of pilot plants such as SaskPower's Boundary Dam power station pilot plant (scale: 4 tCO2 captured/day) [1], the EU project CASTOR pilot plant, the TAURON Laziska pilot plant (scale: 1 tCO2 captured/day) [2], the Brindisi Pilot Plant (scale: 60 tCO2 captured/day) [3] and the Korea Electric Power Research Institute pilot plant (scale: 2 tCO2 captured/day) [4].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although these processes using chemical absorption of CO2 with amine solvents have great potential for removing CO2 they are known to be energy intensive due to the energy required for solvent regeneration [5]. Hence, a large number of studies have
texts.text: Although these processes using chemical absorption of CO2 with amine solvents have great potential for removing CO2 they are known to be energy intensive due to the energy required for solvent regeneration [5]. Hence, a large number of studies have

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aqueous MEA-based CO2 capture is one of main technologies for CO2 capture. However, there are various different sources of fl ue gases from different industries which have different fl ow conditions. In particular the CO2 content is very important and it will have a critical impact on the associated design of CO2 capture processes. In this study a superstructure optimization methodology is applied to ranges of different fl ue gases found in non-power industries such as cement, steel and re fi nery plants. Optimization of both operating conditions and structural modi fi cations reveals the optimal con fi gurations of equipment for the different industrial sources of CO2. A case study is given to address how energy consumption and process design of MEA-based CO2 capture systems is in fl uenced by CO2 concentration in the feed gas. It is shown that fl ue gas splitting is the most signi fi cant and useful process modi fi cation for all the different fl ue gases tested in particular for low CO2 content fl ue gases. At higher CO2 contents the optimal designs are shown to require a combination of process modi fi cations to give an even greater reduction of energy requirements.
texts.text: Aqueous MEA-based CO2 capture is one of main technologies for CO2 capture. However, there are various different sources of fl ue gases from different industries which have different fl ow conditions. In particular the CO2 content is very important and it will have a critical impact on the associated design of CO2 capture processes. In this study a superstructure optimization methodology is applied to ranges of different fl ue gases found in non-power industries such as cement, steel and re fi nery plants. Optimization of both operating conditions and structural modi fi cations reveals the optimal con fi gurations of equipment for the different industrial sources of CO2. A case study is given to address how energy consumption and process design of MEA-based CO2 capture systems is in fl uenced by CO2 concentration in the feed gas. It is shown that fl ue gas splitting is the most signi fi cant and useful process modi fi cation for all the different fl ue gases tested in particular for low CO2 content fl ue gases. At higher CO2 contents the optimal designs are shown to require a combination of process modi fi cations to give an even greater reduction of energy requirements.

texts.orig: For example in the fi rst category Oh et al. [6] consider the conventional con fi guration and a number of different structural modifi cations which are optimized in a superstructure to fi nd the optimal design for a fl ue gas with 3.73% CO2 (molar basis). Additionally, the study of Darmartzis et al. [7] also presents a superstructure approach, although in their work they focus on a small number of con fi gurations which are compared for the purpose of processing a fl ue gas with a fi xed 14.11% CO2 (molar basis). Similarly, Lee et al. [8] consider a 13% CO2 mole fraction but utilize a superstructure optimization including 11 different structural modifi cations. texts.label: text
texts.orig: capturing CO2 from a fl ue gas from cement plant fl ue gases which have a higher but still fi xed 20.4% CO2 (molar basis). In these studies different process modi fi cations are considered and each lead to different recommendations for the best way to reduce the energy consumption of CO2 capture. Oh et al. [6] suggest fl ue gas splitting, Darmartzis et al. [7] indicates absorber intercooling in one of the most important most important modi fi cation, Dubois and Thomas [9] recommends rich/lean vapor compression while Lee et al. [8] identify an optimal solution which combines absorber intercooling and rich vapor compression. Considering these different solutions it seems possible that different structural modifi cations may be appropriate for different feed gas compositions.
texts.text: capturing CO2 from a fl ue gas from cement plant fl ue gases which have a higher but still fi xed 20.4% CO2 (molar basis). In these studies different process modi fi cations are considered and each lead to different recommendations for the best way to reduce the energy consumption of CO2 capture. Oh et al. [6] suggest fl ue gas splitting, Darmartzis et al. [7] indicates absorber intercooling in one of the most important most important modi fi cation, Dubois and Thomas [9] recommends rich/lean vapor compression while Lee et al. [8] identify an optimal solution which combines absorber intercooling and rich vapor compression. Considering these different solutions it seems possible that different structural modifi cations may be appropriate for different feed gas compositions.

texts.orig: In the second category Arias et al. [10] also consider a fi xed conventional con fi guration and through sensitivity analysis they show that CO2 content is one of the most sensitive parameters affecting the total annual costs of CO2 capture. Akram et al. [11] report the results of a pilot plant study considering fl ue gases with CO2 content ranging from 5 to 10% using a fi xed conventional con fi guration together with exhaust gas recycling to enhance the concentration of CO2. Their study fi nds using that con fi guration reboiler duty decreases by approximately 7.1% per percentage increase in CO2 content. Soltani et al. [12] utilize a fi xed equipment

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to studies that fall into the above two categories there have been a more limited number of studies which have considered both different CO2 content and different con fi gurations simultaneously. This includes Diego et al. [13] who compares a conventional amine capture process against an exhaust gas recycle con fi guration utilizing a hybrid absorption-membrane separation to capture CO2. Although it appears that Diego et al. [13] have only considered a single inlet CO2 mole fraction for each con fi guration. As well as this the only study which considers different structural modifi cations for different CO2 contents is that of Oh et al. [14]. However, the study of Oh et al. [14] investigates only narrow ranges of CO2 mole fractions due to part-load operation (3.64 e 4.22% for natural gas and 10.3 e 13.6% for coal fi red power plants).

As well as this the only study which considers different structural modifi cations for different CO2 contents is that of Oh et al. [14]. However, the study of Oh et al. Hence, this study focuses on the systematic design and optimization of such capture processes including different possible structural modi fi cations for a wide range of different fl ue

texts.orig: 2. Process modeling, design and optimization
texts.text: 2. Process modeling, design and optimization

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. CO2 emission sources from non-power industries
texts.text: 2.1. CO2 emission sources from non-power industries

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Seven different fl ue gases are considered in this study, as shown in Table 1. These include fl ue gases generated by fi ve processes from a steel plant including: combustion chamber, lime kiln, sinter plant, utility process and hot stove. A fl ue gas emitted from the calcination process of the cement industry. Additionally, off-gas from a fl uid catalytic cracker in a re fi nery plant.
texts.text: Seven different fl ue gases are considered in this study, as shown in Table 1. These include fl ue gases generated by fi ve processes from a steel plant including: combustion chamber, lime kiln, sinter plant, utility process and hot stove. A fl ue gas emitted from the calcination process of the cement industry. Additionally, off-gas from a fl uid catalytic cracker in a re fi nery plant.

texts.orig: 2.2. Modeling and simulation of superstructure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The commercial simulator Unisim ® is utilized to simulate the superstructure as illustrated in Fig. 1. This superstructure contains the four possible structural modi fi cations suggested by Oh et al. [6]. This includes an absorber ' intercooler ' option which allows a higher absorption rate of CO2 by maintaining lower temperatures in the column. Performance can also be enhanced by splitting either the fl ue gas feed ( ' fl ue gas split ' ) or the solvent feed ( ' solvent

Another option called ' semilean ' involves extracting partially regenerated solvent and feeding it to the absorber. All these four structural modi fi cations have the potential to reduce the regeneration energy required. The process fl ow diagrams simulated in Unisim ® for the conventional CO2 capture process and four potential structural modifi cations are shown in Fig. 2. Unisim ® has been widely used to simulate aminebased CO2 capture processes because of its specialized Amine

texts.orig: To validate the simulation model results are compared against pilot plant data [19]. In this pilot plant, fl ue gas from a natural gas - fi red burner is used and its gas fl owrate is 2.6 t/day. The simulation is made with feed conditions and other design speci fi cations, assuming no heat losses in any of the equipment, which are shown in Table 2. A comparison of pilot plant data and simulation results is made in Table 3, which shows a good agreement. Hence, the process modeling and simulation framework is validated.
texts.text: To validate the simulation model results are compared against pilot plant data [19]. In this pilot plant, fl ue gas from a natural gas - fi red burner is used and its gas fl owrate is 2.6 t/day. The simulation is made with feed conditions and other design speci fi cations, assuming no heat losses in any of the equipment, which are shown in Table 2. A comparison of pilot plant data and simulation results is made in Table 3, which shows a good agreement. Hence, the process modeling and simulation framework is validated.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Scale-up design
texts.text: 2.3. Scale-up design

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To deal with commercial scale CO2 capture from fl ue gases, scale-up design is carried out. The simulation validated at the pilot plant scale is adjusted and recalculated for larger scales. The conventional fl owsheet, as given in Fig. 2(a), is simulated for the validation of scale-up design. In this study Mellapak 250Y ® structured packing from Sulzer Chemtech Ltd. is selected for use in both the absorber and a stripper, which is one of the commonly-used packings for absorption processes. The column diameter is calculated with correlation data that is based on experiment [20], when fl ooding level is taken at about 80% of the maximum hydraulic capacity which is a normal operating point. Height is calculated by using Unisim ® utility calculator and is validated with Agbonghae et al. [21]. As validation procedure, the case of 400 MWe NGCC is selected and its column design reported in the reference [21] is compared. The absorber height required for 90% CO2 capture is calculated with different L/G ratios at constant lean loading by using simulation speci fi cations given and this procedure is repeated with four cases of lean loadings (i.e. 0.15, 0.2, 0.25 and 0.3). As shown in Fig. 3, the simulation results tested at commercial scale have good agreement with results reported in the reference [21].
texts.text: To deal with commercial scale CO2 capture from fl ue gases, scale-up design is carried out. The simulation validated at the pilot plant scale is adjusted and recalculated for larger scales. The conventional fl owsheet, as given in Fig. 2(a), is simulated for the validation of scale-up design. In this study Mellapak 250Y ® structured packing from Sulzer Chemtech Ltd. is selected for use in both the absorber and a stripper, which is one of the commonly-used packings for absorption processes. The column diameter is calculated with correlation data that is based on experiment [20], when fl ooding level is taken at about 80% of the maximum hydraulic capacity which is a normal operating point. Height is calculated by using Unisim ® utility calculator and is validated with Agbonghae et al. [21]. As validation procedure, the case of 400 MWe NGCC is selected and its column design reported in the reference [21] is compared. The absorber height required for 90% CO2 capture is calculated with different L/G ratios at constant lean loading by using simulation speci fi cations given and this procedure is repeated with four cases of lean loadings (i.e. 0.15, 0.2, 0.25 and 0.3). As shown in Fig. 3, the simulation results tested at commercial scale have good agreement with results reported in the reference [21].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Additionally the simulation approach is validated against commercial-scale simulation results [22], as given in Table 4, which veri fi es the applicability of the scale-up simulation model designed in this study.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: variables are speci fi ed within MATLAB ® which are passed to Unisim ® for the simulation. Simulation results are returned back to MATLAB ® with which the objective function is calculated and its optimality is assessed with in-built optimization algorithms. This procedure between MATLAB ® and Unisim ® is repeated until optimal value is calculated. A typical simulation inside Unisim ® required 1 e 5 s and subsequent optimization required 3 e 5 h using typical settings inside MATLAB ® . Genetic Algorithm (GA) is used for the optimization, due to its effectiveness in fi nding quasi-optimal conditions for multivariable problems [23].

texts.text: J. Choi et al. / Energy 185 (2019) 971 e 980

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The concept of superstructure for the process synthesis is to consider all the possible structural options. However, consideration of all the options demands considerably high computational resources and efforts, because of high non-linearity in fl owsheet simulation. Hence, sensitivity analysis has been used to identify promising candidates and the to select options with their bounds, as shown in Table 5. Also, engineering judgement is also made on the selection of operating conditions or bounds. For example, the maximum fl owrate allowed for intercooling and semi-lean stream is set up to 50% of internal liquid fl owrate in the absorber.
texts.text: The concept of superstructure for the process synthesis is to consider all the possible structural options. However, consideration of all the options demands considerably high computational resources and efforts, because of high non-linearity in fl owsheet simulation. Hence, sensitivity analysis has been used to identify promising candidates and the to select options with their bounds, as shown in Table 5. Also, engineering judgement is also made on the selection of operating conditions or bounds. For example, the maximum fl owrate allowed for intercooling and semi-lean stream is set up to 50% of internal liquid fl owrate in the absorber.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For superstructure optimization, the objective function is set as overall energy cost per ton of CO2, subject to minimum 90% of CO2 recovery.
texts.text: For superstructure optimization, the objective function is set as overall energy cost per ton of CO2, subject to minimum 90% of CO2 recovery.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: steam and cooling water, respectively and Ce, CLP and Ccw are overall consumption of electricity, LP steam and cooling water, respectively. Electricity is used to drive pumps, while LP steam is required in the reboiler. RCO2 is CO2 recovery rate in t/h. Ye is the value of CO2 recovery (90%). The objective function is penalized when 90% of CO2 recovery is not met. For this study, $ 0.06/kWh for the unit cost of electricity, $ 0.05/kWh for LP steam, and $0.001/kWh for cooling water is taken, which are based on the reference [24]. Typical tuning parameters used for Genetic Algorithm in this work are as follows: vector for population type; 200 for population size; 100 multiplied with the number of variables for the maximum number of generations; rank for scaling function; scattered for creation function; stochastic uniform for selection function; 0.05 from the population size for elite count; 0.8 for crossover fraction; 0.2 for migration fraction; 20 for migration interval.
texts.text: steam and cooling water, respectively and Ce, CLP and Ccw are overall consumption of electricity, LP steam and cooling water, respectively. Electricity is used to drive pumps, while LP steam is required in the reboiler. RCO2 is CO2 recovery rate in t/h. Ye is the value of CO2 recovery (90%). The objective function is penalized when 90% of CO2 recovery is not met. For this study, $ 0.06/kWh for the unit cost of electricity, $ 0.05/kWh for LP steam, and $0.001/kWh for cooling water is taken, which are based on the reference [24]. Typical tuning parameters used for Genetic Algorithm in this work are as follows: vector for population type; 200 for population size; 100 multiplied with the number of variables for the maximum number of generations; rank for scaling function; scattered for creation function; stochastic uniform for selection function; 0.05 from the population size for elite count; 0.8 for crossover fraction; 0.2 for migration fraction; 20 for migration interval.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Results and discussion
texts.text: 3. Results and discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study aims not only to minimize reboiler duty through systematic analysis of superstructure modi fi cations for MEA-based CO2 capture process, but also to understand impacts of different CO2 mole fractions on the design of absorption-desorption processes. Initially a conventional amine-based con fi guration is optimized for each of the 7 different fl ues gases considered (with different CO2% mole fractions). The results shown in Table 6 show that lower reboiler duties can be obtained when processing fl ue gases with a higher CO2 mole fraction. This is in agreement with the fi ndings of Akram et al. [11]. Subsequently the superstructure of possible structural modifi cations is optimized for each of the different fl ue gases to obtain further reduction of energy requirements. The optimal results for conventional and superstructure-based capture processes used for the FCC fl ue gas are shown in Fig. 4. It can be seen that the superstructure with process modifi cations is able to achieve a 10.0% reduction of reboiler duty compared to the 6.1% reduction that can be achieved with the conventional con fi guration. Optimal results of other cases are summarized in Tables 6 and 7. It should be noted that some cases in Table 7 do not meet the required CO2 removal rate from the superstructure optimization. This is related to computational diffi culties in the optimization in which complex design interactions
texts.text: This study aims not only to minimize reboiler duty through systematic analysis of superstructure modi fi cations for MEA-based CO2 capture process, but also to understand impacts of different CO2 mole fractions on the design of absorption-desorption processes. Initially a conventional amine-based con fi guration is optimized for each of the 7 different fl ues gases considered (with different CO2% mole fractions). The results shown in Table 6 show that lower reboiler duties can be obtained when processing fl ue gases with a higher CO2 mole fraction. This is in agreement with the fi ndings of Akram et al. [11]. Subsequently the superstructure of possible structural modifi cations is optimized for each of the different fl ue gases to obtain further reduction of energy requirements. The optimal results for conventional and superstructure-based capture processes used for the FCC fl ue gas are shown in Fig. 4. It can be seen that the superstructure with process modifi cations is able to achieve a 10.0% reduction of reboiler duty compared to the 6.1% reduction that can be achieved with the conventional con fi guration. Optimal results of other cases are summarized in Tables 6 and 7. It should be noted that some cases in Table 7 do not meet the required CO2 removal rate from the superstructure optimization. This is related to computational diffi culties in the optimization in which complex design interactions

texts.text: Table 7 Optimization results based on a superstructure fl owsheet.

texts.orig: are existed in the fl owsheet simulation, and the recovery constraint is relaxed from 90% to 89% when considerably large computation time is required or there is a great deal of dif fi culties in obtaining converged simulation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Using these optimal designs the minimum energy costs for all the cases considered are shown in Fig. 5 which shows that significant energy reductions can be achieved at all conditions, in particular cases where there is higher CO2 content in the fl ue gas. Compared to conventional con fi guration, the superstructure-based optimal con fi gurations are all shown to include all of the four potential structural modi fi cations simultaneously in order to obtain the lowest energy requirements.
texts.text: Using these optimal designs the minimum energy costs for all the cases considered are shown in Fig. 5 which shows that significant energy reductions can be achieved at all conditions, in particular cases where there is higher CO2 content in the fl ue gas. Compared to conventional con fi guration, the superstructure-based optimal con fi gurations are all shown to include all of the four potential structural modi fi cations simultaneously in order to obtain the lowest energy requirements.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to consider the in fl uence of each individual structural modifi cation a new factor called the ' relative utilization ' is de fi ned which calculates a rough estimate of the contribution of each individual modi fi cation to the overall con fi guration which achieves the lowest energy consumption. The methods of calculation for each modi fi cation suggested here are given in Table 8. If the relative utilization is small (e.g. if the draw rate of intercooling is small) then the removal of that modi fi cation would likely have only a small effect on the achieved energy reductions. The calculated relative utilizations are shown both in Table 8 and in Fig. 6. Looking at these values it can be seen that fl ue gas splitting is a signi fi cant component of the optimal designs for all the conditions tested. In particular at low CO2 mole fractions this structural modi fi cation appears to be the main component which is contributing to the achieved energy reductions on its own. At higher CO2 mole fractions the other structural modi fi cations are shown to become more signi fi cant parts of the optimal designs indicating that to achieve the highest energy reductions using high CO2 content feed utilizing a combination of all four modi fi cations becomes bene fi cial. The speci fi c reboiler duties of the optimal results identi fi ed from this case study are compared with various results reported in the references [8,15,16,25 e 28] (Fig. 7), which validates the soundness and effectiveness of the process design and optimization framework proposed.
texts.text: In order to consider the in fl uence of each individual structural modifi cation a new factor called the ' relative utilization ' is de fi ned which calculates a rough estimate of the contribution of each individual modi fi cation to the overall con fi guration which achieves the lowest energy consumption. The methods of calculation for each modi fi cation suggested here are given in Table 8. If the relative utilization is small (e.g. if the draw rate of intercooling is small) then the removal of that modi fi cation would likely have only a small effect on the achieved energy reductions. The calculated relative utilizations are shown both in Table 8 and in Fig. 6. Looking at these values it can be seen that fl ue gas splitting is a signi fi cant component of the optimal designs for all the conditions tested. In particular at low CO2 mole fractions this structural modi fi cation appears to be the main component which is contributing to the achieved energy reductions on its own. At higher CO2 mole fractions the other structural modi fi cations are shown to become more signi fi cant parts of the optimal designs indicating that to achieve the highest energy reductions using high CO2 content feed utilizing a combination of all four modi fi cations becomes bene fi cial. The speci fi c reboiler duties of the optimal results identi fi ed from this case study are compared with various results reported in the references [8,15,16,25 e 28] (Fig. 7), which validates the soundness and effectiveness of the process design and optimization framework proposed.

texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study focuses on the determination of optimal designs for
texts.text: This study focuses on the determination of optimal designs for

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: J. Choi et al. / Energy 185 (2019) 971 e 980
texts.text: J. Choi et al. / Energy 185 (2019) 971 e 980

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MEA-based CO2 capture process applied to fl ue gases with a wide range of different CO2 content originating from non-power industries including steel, cement and re fi nery plants. In particular seven different fl ue gases are selected from these three industries and a superstructure based optimization methodology is used to identify structural modi fi cations which lead to the greatest reduction of energy consumption for each fl ue gas. The seven different fl ues gases include a wide range of CO2 contents (4.8% e 27.5% CO2 mole fractions). In agreement with the fi ndings of Akram et al. [11] and Soltani et al. [12] it is found that increasing the CO2 content reduces the energy requirements of this kind of CO2 capture method. Further to this fi nding it is also observed here that the energy reduction resulting from the addition of structural modi fi cations is also enhanced with higher CO2 content (as shown in 5) with increasing energy savings possible at higher CO2 content compared to the conventional con fi guration.
texts.text: MEA-based CO2 capture process applied to fl ue gases with a wide range of different CO2 content originating from non-power industries including steel, cement and re fi nery plants. In particular seven different fl ue gases are selected from these three industries and a superstructure based optimization methodology is used to identify structural modi fi cations which lead to the greatest reduction of energy consumption for each fl ue gas. The seven different fl ues gases include a wide range of CO2 contents (4.8% e 27.5% CO2 mole fractions). In agreement with the fi ndings of Akram et al. [11] and Soltani et al. [12] it is found that increasing the CO2 content reduces the energy requirements of this kind of CO2 capture method. Further to this fi nding it is also observed here that the energy reduction resulting from the addition of structural modi fi cations is also enhanced with higher CO2 content (as shown in 5) with increasing energy savings possible at higher CO2 content compared to the conventional con fi guration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Application of the superstructure methodology has in all cases resulted in solutions which combine all four of the individual process modi fi cations considered here. For this reason we have calculated the ' relative utilization ' of each individual process modifi cation in each of the optimal designs. This is a simple method which shows the relative importance/signi fi cance/usage of the individual modi fi cations in the overall designs. It is shown in Table 8 and Fig. 6 that fl ue gas splitting is the most important process

It is shown in Table 8 and Fig. 6 that fl ue gas splitting is the most important process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: modifi cations for all conditions, in particular for cases with low CO2 content fl ue gases. Moving towards higher CO2 content all four modifi cations become more prominent with optimal solutions utilizing all four modi fi cations. This tells us that at low CO2 content a single process modi fi cation ( fl ue gas splitting) may be suf fi cient to achieve a signi fi cant reduction of energy requirements. At higher CO2 contents greater energy reductions can be realized using a combination of fl ue gas splitting plus one or more of the other modifi cations.
texts.text: modifi cations for all conditions, in particular for cases with low CO2 content fl ue gases. Moving towards higher CO2 content all four modifi cations become more prominent with optimal solutions utilizing all four modi fi cations. This tells us that at low CO2 content a single process modi fi cation ( fl ue gas splitting) may be suf fi cient to achieve a signi fi cant reduction of energy requirements. At higher CO2 contents greater energy reductions can be realized using a combination of fl ue gas splitting plus one or more of the other modifi cations.
